title: Analyze the current x-auto architecture for multi-reply citation support. Focus on the best schema/source-of-truth design for Notion plus queue plus scheduler. Evaluate at least 3 options (e.g. multi-line Source URLs reuse, new Notion rich_text/url property, JSON property, relation-based child rows)...
summary_text: Here is the read-only analysis of the current x-auto citation architecture and a minimal-viable recommendation. ## Current architecture (as implemented today) - `PostRecord` carries both `source_url` and `reply_url` as plain strings, with `reply_url` used as the runtime reply-chain payload. - [schema.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/schema.py:41) - Notion sync currently treats `"Source URLs"` as a single `url` property and maps it only to `source_url` on read. - Read: [notion_sync.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/notion_sync.py:247) - Write: [notion_sync.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/notion_sync.py:343) - `reply_url` is **not** a first-class Notion field in sync; it survives only if present in local cache and merged back into Notion rows. - Cache-supplement behavior in `scheduler._post_due_jobs`: [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:450), [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:445) - Runtime posting uses `reply_url` or fallback `source_url` for reply chain, then splits by lines and posts sequential replies. - [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:571), [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:575), [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:633) - `apply_post_package` currently accepts citation arrays but flattens them into `reply_url` as `\n`-joined text; only `source_url` is written to Notion as `"Source URLs"`. - [scripts/apply_post_package.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scripts/apply_post_package.py:73), [scripts/apply_post_package.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scripts/apply_post_package.py:77), [scripts/apply_post_package.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scripts/apply_post_package.py:19) Implication: the system is already “conceptually multi-reply capable” in scheduler/queue, but Notion is only single-URL SoT for citations, creating drift risk. --- ## Option analysis (3+ options) ### 1) Multi-line reuse of existing `Source URLs` property (keep name/type unchanged) - How it looks: encode multiple links into one string field (e.g., newline-delimited) and keep using one property. - Why it is weak: - Notion `url` property semantics are single URL + strict validation, so multiline content is at best undefined/fragile. - Current read/write code already assumes `url` field object shape. - parse/write sites: [notion_sync.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/notion_sync.py:247), [notion_sync.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/notion_sync.py:343) - Minimal code changes: low, but breakage risk and runtime parser ambiguity are high. ### 2) Add dedicated Notion `Reply URLs` (rich_text/Plain text) + keep `source_url` in sync - How it looks: introduce a new string field for ordered multi-URL payload, keep `source_url` as single canonical URL. - Why this is the best minimal option: - Keeps existing behavior in scheduler/queue almost unchanged. - Avoids changing old property semantics. - Explicitly restores Notion as source of truth for reply chains (current gap). - Easy migration path: fallback to `source_url` when `Reply URLs` missing. - Concrete touchpoints needed: - Notion read: [notion_sync.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/notion_sync.py:247) - Notion write: [notion_sync.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/notion_sync.py:318) - Ingest/push path from generator bundle: [scripts/apply_post_package.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scripts/apply_post_package.py:73) - Scheduler uses `reply_url` field; just normalize from Notion-sourced `Reply URLs` and preserve offline merge path: [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:450), [scheduler.py](/Users/masayuki/Dev/x-auto/scheduler.py:571) ### 3) JSON-in-text property in Notion (single string containing JSON array) - How it looks: store `["https://...","https://..."]` in a text/rich_text property. - Pros: - Can carry ordered list in one field. - Can add metadata later via inline object shape. - Cons: - Non-human-friendly for editors. - Needs strict JSON parse and error handling; fragile with manual edits. - More code complexit...
